title: Legal predator control on a smallholding
subtitle: What you can and cannot do to protect livestock from foxes, corvids, and raptors.
excerpt: The law on predator control in the UK is specific and the boundaries between legal and illegal are not obvious. Shooting foxes is legal under the right conditions; poisoning anything is not. Birds of prey are fully protected; corvids may be controlled under licences that change regularly. Knowing the framework keeps you on the right side of it.
sourceNotes: Wildlife and Countryside Act 1981 (as amended). Natural England General Licences for crow control, gov.uk. BASC (British Association for Shooting and Conservation) fox control guidance. GWCT (Game & Wildlife Conservation Trust) predator management notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A licence issued by Natural England (or equivalent devolved authority) that allows named activities — such as controlling specific corvid species — without a named individual permit. Available to anyone who qualifies; you don't apply for one individually.
Killing an animal in a manner that results in rapid unconsciousness and death without pain. A legal requirement for all predator control.

content.text: Protecting livestock from predators is legal. The method matters. UK law sets specific routes for predator control. Anything outside them is a wildlife crime.

Foxes are not a protected species in England. You may shoot a fox on land you have permission to shoot on, at any time of day or night, using a suitable firearm. You must ensure the shot can be taken safely (clear backstop, no risk to people or livestock) and that the fox is killed quickly and humanely.
Live cage traps may be set for foxes; any fox caught in a live trap must be killed

, it cannot be relocated and released. It is an offence to cause unnecessary suffering to a trapped fox by leaving it without regular checks. Live traps must be checked at least once every twenty-four hours.
Certain corvid species, carrion crow, rook, jackdaw, jay, magpie, may be controlled under

issued by Natural England. The licences specify the purposes for which control is permitted, protecting livestock and crops are among them, and the methods allowed. Shooting and cage trapping are permitted; other methods may not be.

General licence terms change. Always read the current version at naturengland.org.uk before taking any action. The 2019 revocation of general licences led to prosecutions. Not knowing the current rules is not a defence.
Poisoning any wild animal, including foxes and corvids. There is no legal poison available to the public for wild mammal control in the UK.
Snaring foxes in Scotland (banned 2024). In England and Wales, snaring is still legal but strictly regulated; self-locking snares are illegal.

Disturbing, destroying, or taking any bird of prey or their nests. Buzzards, red kites, sparrowhawks, kestrels, and all other raptors are fully protected under Schedule 1 of the Wildlife and Countryside Act. Shooting or trapping them is a serious criminal offence.
Using any spring trap not approved for the target species.

The most effective predator control for a small holding is physical: fox-proof fencing or netting, pop-hole openers with reliable timers, and a solid coop rather than a flimsy one. Lethal control reduces the local predator population temporarily; prevention reduces the attack opportunities permanently.
